personal_info.name: Anway Bose
personal_info.headline: Ph.D. Candidate in Electrical and Computer Engineering | Quantitative Researcher

summary: Quantitative Researcher and Ph.D. Candidate in Electrical and Computer Engineering at Temple University with a strong foundation in probability theory, statistical modeling, and optimization. Extensive experience designing mathematically rigorous models for inference, forecasting, and decision-making in complex systems. Proven ability to work with large datasets, develop scalable statistical pipelines in Python, and translate theoretical frameworks into deployable computational models. Research focuses on federated optimization methods, multi-agent robotics, distributed control, and secure test beds for Distributed AI.

experience.description: Working on Embodied AI & Multi-Agent Spatial Reasoning, Privacy-Preserving Distributed Optimization, and Reinforcement Learning-Based Control.
highlights: Developed multi-agent learning frameworks for distributed perception and decision-making in spatially distributed robotic systems.
Designed optimization-based consensus frameworks for globally consistent 3D direction-of-arrival estimation.
Developed a fully decentralized homomorphic federated learning framework using CKKS encryption, eliminating centralized aggregation.
Designed reinforcement learning pipelines integrating representation learning and inverse kinematics.
Engineered modular robotics data acquisition systems streaming synchronized EMG and IMU data into ROS2 topics.

experience.description: Developed and analyzed a Model Predictive Controller design for Permanent Magnet Linear Synchronous Motors used in Ball Bonder machines.
highlights: Developed and validated real-time control algorithms for industrial electromechanical systems.
Conducted experimental validation under latency and stability constraints.
Implemented hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) testing pipelines to verify controller performance under realistic operating conditions.
Developed reusable evaluation pipelines for system benchmarking.
Conducted recitations, developed problem sets, and mentored graduate students in control theory and optimization.
Assisted in courses: Control Systems, Circuits and Electronics, Digital Circuit Design, Engineering Analysis, and Engineering Programming.

experience.description: Focused on Mobile Robotics, Large-Scale Optimization, and SLAM.
highlights: Implemented Gauss-Newton, Levenberg-Marquardt, EM, and EKF-based estimators.
Developed scalable successive convex approximation methods for multi-agent estimation.
Studied convergence and computational trade-offs in nonlinear optimization pipelines.
